text: During the laparoscopic excision of the mesh, the surgeon identifies an inferior folded edge of the mesh. The examination of the abdominal wall shows no scar tissue or spigellian hernia at the patient's indicated area of pain. Exploration of the posterior rectus fascia reveals no visible pathology such as neuroma or intramuscular lipoma.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision laterally and inferiorly to create a flap, exposing the inguinal region and the mesh. Monopolar cautery is used to maintain hemostasis, allowing clear visualization of the anatomical planes and structures.

The surgeon exposes the inferior edge of the mesh with a second peritoneal incision, connecting it to the previous incision to complete the flap. The dissection of the mesh is carried medially using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, with electrocautery applied as needed.

The surgeon mobilizes the inferior clam-shelled edge of the mesh from the myobactinial orifice, encountering a cord lipoma. The lipoma is excised as a potential source of pain. The vas deferens and cord structures are identified and carefully mobilized free from the mesh using sharp dissection.
